title: Surveillance of the Patient Who Has Had Therapeutic Hemithyroidectomy for Thyroid Cancer

items.title: Why neck ultrasonography is primary after hemithyroidectomy
items.text: After hemithyroidectomy for low-risk differentiated thyroid cancer, surveillance is primarily with neck ultrasonography; optimal long-term imaging frequency remains unknown.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Imaging surveillance after hemithyroidectomy is primarily with neck ultrasonography. Optimal frequency of follow-up is unknown."

items.title: How dynamic risk stratification differs after hemithyroidectomy
items.text: Modified dynamic risk stratification uses nonstimulated thyroglobulin less than 30 ng/mL to define excellent response—different from total thyroidectomy thresholds; trend over time matters more than a single value.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "modified dynamic risk stratification has been developed, which has a thyroglobulin threshold of less than 30 ng/mL (<30 μg/L) to define an excellent response to therapy"

items.title: Why detectable thyroglobulin does not always mean recurrence
items.text: Thyroglobulin is produced by normal and malignant thyroid tissue; detectable levels after lobectomy may reflect remnant normal tissue, and levels can rise ~10% per year without recurrence.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Detectable thyroglobulin does not always indicate recurrence of thyroid cancer, and it may indicate the presence of normal thyroid tissue."

items.title: How often patients require thyroid hormone after lobectomy
items.text: Many patients choose hemithyroidectomy to avoid lifelong levothyroxine, but 50% to 71% require thyroid hormone to achieve goal TSH less than 2.0 mIU/L for most response categories.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "studies have shown that in order for patients to attain goal TSH (<2.0 mIU/L for most patients based on response to therapy), 50% to 71% of patients require some amount of thyroid hormone therapy."

items.title: Why preoperative neck ultrasonography is essential
items.text: Thorough preoperative imaging of thyroid and central/lateral neck guides initial surgery extent and establishes baseline for postoperative surveillance; missing preoperative imaging should be completed postoperatively.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Thorough preoperative imaging of both the thyroid and central and lateral neck compartments is essential when evaluating persons with suspicious thyroid nodules"

items.title: Pitfall: over-monitoring from false-positive surveillance ultrasound
items.text: False-positive findings on surveillance ultrasonography occur in 57% to 67% of low- and intermediate-risk patients, risking unnecessary procedures.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "studies have shown that false-positive findings on surveillance ultrasonography can occur in 57% to 67% of patients at low and intermediate risk."

items.title: How thyroglobulin antibodies interfere with tumor marker use
items.text: Thyroglobulin antibodies interfere with the immunoassay and confound interpretation of serum thyroglobulin as a tumor marker—use the same assay serially when monitoring.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "The presence of thyroglobulin antibodies interferes with the thyroglobulin immunoassay and interferes with the interpretation of serum thyroglobulin as a tumor marker."

items.title: How to manage rising thyroglobulin with negative neck imaging after lobectomy
items.text: With negative neck imaging and TSH above goal 0.5–2.0 mIU/L off levothyroxine, start low-dose levothyroxine; if opposed to hormone, short-interval surveillance with consistent Tg assay is reasonable.
items.reference: Case 1: "Therefore, starting low-dosage levothyroxine (Answer C) is recommended, with follow-up measurement of TSH, thyroglobulin, and thyroglobulin antibodies."

items.title: What surveillance is appropriate after low-risk FVPTC hemithyroidectomy
items.text: For low-risk follicular variant papillary carcinoma with negative contralateral lobe and nodes: neck US at 6 months, TSH measurement with levothyroxine if TSH above goal, and selective thyroglobulin monitoring.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: A, B, and possibly C) TSH measurement and thyroid hormone therapy if TSH is >2.0 mIU/L, neck ultrasonography, and possibly measurement of thyroglobulin and thyroglobulin antibodies"

items.title: Why hobnail papillary carcinoma requires completion thyroidectomy
items.text: Hobnail variants are ATA intermediate-risk aggressive histology associated with extrathyroidal extension and nodal metastases—completion thyroidectomy and possible radioactive iodine are recommended.
items.reference: Case 4: "Hobnail variants are in the American Thyroid Association intermediate risk of recurrence category and are considered to be aggressive histologic types."

items.title: How extranodal extension changes management after lobectomy
items.text: Extranodal extension is a risk factor for structural recurrence; with central neck nodal metastasis and extranodal extension, completion thyroidectomy with central neck dissection is preferred.
items.reference: Case 5: "Extranodal extension is considered a risk factor for structural disease recurrence"

items.title: When angioinvasive follicular carcinoma requires completion therapy
items.text: High-risk angioinvasive follicular thyroid carcinoma is an indication for adjuvant radioactive iodine at diagnosis; continued surveillance alone is inadequate—many clinicians choose completion thyroidectomy with adjuvant RAI.
items.reference: Case 2: "High-risk angioinvasive follicular thyroid carcinoma is an indication for adjuvant radioactive iodine therapy at the time of diagnosis."

items.question: One year after left lobectomy for 1.2-cm papillary carcinoma, TSH 2.7 mIU/L off levothyroxine, stable 4-mm right spongiform nodule, Tg 75 ng/mL on different assay. Best next step?
options: Observation with US, Tg, TgAb, and TFT in 6 months
items.explanation: Negative neck imaging with TSH above goal favors starting low-dose levothyroxine; if hormone-averse, Answer D with same Tg assay is acceptable.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C or D) Initiation of levothyroxine, 50 mcg daily or continued observation"

items.question: After hemithyroidectomy, pathology reveals high-risk angioinvasive follicular thyroid carcinoma. Best management?
options: Completion thyroidectomy with adjuvant radioactive iodine
items.explanation: Angioinvasive follicular carcinoma warrants completion thyroidectomy and adjuvant RAI; surveillance alone is inadequate.
items.reference: Case 2: "many clinicians would choose completion thyroidectomy with adjuvant radioactive iodine (Answer B)"

items.question: Six months after hemithyroidectomy for 1.8-cm encapsulated FVPTC without vascular invasion, appropriate actions include:
options: TSH measurement and levothyroxine if TSH >2.0 mIU/L
items.explanation: Answers A, B, and possibly E—low-risk disease does not need completion therapy or chest CT.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: A, B, and possibly C)"

items.question: Post-lobectomy hobnail variant papillary carcinoma. Recommended now?
options: Completion thyroidectomy and consideration of RAI
items.explanation: Aggressive hobnail histology warrants completion thyroidectomy and possible RAI; observation is not preferred.
items.reference: Case 4: "Answer: E) Completion thyroidectomy and consideration of radioactive iodine"

items.question: After right lobectomy for toxic nodule, pathology shows papillary carcinoma with 9-mm nodal metastasis and extranodal extension without formal central neck dissection. Best next step?
options: Observation with US and Tg in 6–12 weeks
Completion thyroidectomy with central neck dissection
items.explanation: T2N1a with extranodal extension and incomplete nodal staging requires completion thyroidectomy with central neck dissection and consideration of RAI.
items.reference: Case 5: "Answer: C) Completion thyroidectomy with central neck dissection"

items.question: After hemithyroidectomy with excellent response (Tg <30 ng/mL), TSH goal is:
items.explanation: Modified dynamic risk stratification sets TSH 0.5–2.0 mIU/L for excellent response after hemithyroidectomy.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Nonstimulated thyroglobulin <30 ng/mL (SI: <30 μg/L) ... TSH Goals ... 0.5-2.0 mIU/L"

items.question: Indeterminate response after lobectomy (stable/declining TgAb, Tg not defining) targets TSH:
items.explanation: Indeterminate response category uses TSH 0.1–0.5 mIU/L per modified hemithyroidectomy table.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Stable or declining thyroglobulin antibody levels ... 0.1-0.5 mIU/L"

items.question: If hemithyroidectomy pathology were NIFT-P with normal contralateral lobe and nodes:
options: No routine Tg; maintain normal TSH without levothyroxine goal <2.0
items.explanation: NIFT-P has very low malignant potential; many clinicians omit routine Tg and do not treat to TSH <2.0.
items.reference: Case 3 (Continued): "treating with levothyroxine for a goal TSH less than 2.0 IU/L is not necessary."

items.question: Microscopic vascular invasion on lobectomy pathology implies:
options: Intermediate ATA recurrence risk; consider completion thyroidectomy ± RAI
items.explanation: Vascular invasion classifies intermediate risk (~15%–30% structural recurrence); completion thyroidectomy should be considered.
items.reference: Case 3 (Continued): "If the tumor had vascular invasion, then it would be classified at intermediate risk of structural recurrence according to the American Thyroid Association classification (approximately 15%-30%)."

items.question: Before hemithyroidectomy, clinicians should discuss:
options: Postoperative surveillance, Tg monitoring, and possibility of needing levothyroxine
That levothyroxine is never needed after lobectomy
items.explanation: Preoperative discussion must cover surveillance plan, bilateral nodularity implications, and realistic levothyroxine expectations.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "There should also be discussion regarding preference for thyroid hormone therapy. Many patients assume they will not need thyroid hormone if they have a hemithyroidectomy."

items.question: If thyroglobulin rises after lobectomy, next step includes:
options: Neck ultrasonography and consider TSH suppression to 0.1–0.5 mIU/L
items.explanation: Rising Tg prompts neck imaging and may warrant levothyroxine adjustment toward indeterminate-response TSH goals.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "If thyroglobulin is rising, the neck should be imaged with ultrasonography."

items.question: Completion thyroidectomy is recommended when initial hemithyroidectomy patient:
options: Meets criteria for radioactive iodine therapy
items.explanation: If criteria for RAI are met, completion thyroidectomy ± central neck dissection is recommended.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "If a patient meets criteria for radioactive iodine, then completion thyroidectomy ± central neck dissection is recommended."

items.question: When serially monitoring thyroglobulin after lobectomy:
options: The same assay should be used each time
Tg is never useful after lobectomy
items.explanation: Tg values vary between assays and must be evaluated using the same assay every time.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Thyroglobulin values can vary between assays, so they must be evaluated using the same assay every time."

items.statement: Imaging surveillance after hemithyroidectomy is primarily performed with neck ultrasonography.
items.explanation: ATA approach emphasizes neck US as the main surveillance modality after lobectomy.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Imaging surveillance after hemithyroidectomy is primarily with neck ultrasonography."

items.statement: Nonstimulated thyroglobulin less than 30 ng/mL defines excellent response after hemithyroidectomy.
items.explanation: Modified dynamic risk stratification uses this threshold distinct from total thyroidectomy criteria.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "a thyroglobulin threshold of less than 30 ng/mL (<30 μg/L) to define an excellent response to therapy"

items.statement: More than half of patients may need levothyroxine after lobectomy to reach goal TSH.
items.explanation: 50%–71% require thyroid hormone to achieve TSH goals in studies cited.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "50% to 71% of patients require some amount of thyroid hormone therapy."

items.statement: A rising thyroglobulin over time is more predictive of recurrence than a single stable value.
items.explanation: Trend analysis outweighs isolated measurements; gradual rise without recurrence can still occur.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "A rising thyroglobulin level over time is more predictive of recurrence than one that is stable or dropping."

items.statement: If a patient does not take thyroid hormone after lobectomy, TSH elevation may raise thyroglobulin without cancer recurrence.
items.explanation: Lack of levothyroxine can increase Tg from remnant tissue stimulation.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "If a patient does not take thyroid hormone, this may cause a rise in thyroglobulin without recurrence."

items.statement: In Case 1, FNA of a stable subcentimeter very-low-risk spongiform nodule is recommended.
items.explanation: Stable low-risk subcentimeter nodule does not meet FNA criteria; neck imaging is negative.
items.reference: Case 1: "This is a low-risk nodule sonographically and it is subcentimeter in size. Hence, FNA (Answer B) would not be recommended."

items.statement: Observation alone is preferred for hobnail variant papillary carcinoma after initial lobectomy.
items.explanation: Higher recurrence risk mandates completion thyroidectomy and possible RAI.
items.reference: Case 4: "Observation (Answer D) is not preferred because the potential higher risk of recurrence."

items.statement: 131I whole-body scan is recommended before completion thyroidectomy with remaining thyroid lobe.
items.explanation: Whole-body scan is not recommended before completion when a thyroid lobe remains.
items.reference: Case 2: "131 I whole-body scan (Answer D) would not be recommended before completion thyroidectomy."

items.assertion: Assertion: Excellent response after hemithyroidectomy includes nonstimulated Tg <30 ng/mL.
items.reason: Reason: Because this threshold is identical to total thyroidectomy plus RAI criteria.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—hemithyroidectomy uses modified thresholds.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Dynamic risk stratification after hemithyroidectomy has different thyroglobulin thresholds that define response to therapy as compared with thresholds of persons who have undergone total thyroidectomy."

items.assertion: Assertion: Many hemithyroidectomy patients ultimately need levothyroxine.
items.reason: Reason: Because 50% to 71% require hormone to achieve goal TSH.
items.explanation: Both true and directly linked in cited studies.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "50% to 71% of patients require some amount of thyroid hormone therapy."

items.assertion: Assertion: Different thyroglobulin assay limits direct comparison to prior values.
items.reason: Reason: Because thyroglobulin assays are standardized internationally with no variation.
items.explanation: Assertion true per Case 1; reason false—assay variation is a known barrier.
items.reference: Case 1: "Thyroglobulin was measured using a different assay, making it difficult to directly compare"

items.assertion: Assertion: Serial neck ultrasound may identify false-positive findings in low-risk patients.
items.reason: Reason: Because ultrasound has no role after hemithyroidectomy.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—US is primary surveillance but can overcall disease.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "false-positive findings on surveillance ultrasonography can occur in 57% to 67% of patients at low and intermediate risk."

items.assertion: Assertion: Extranodal extension increases structural recurrence risk.
items.reason: Reason: Because it mandates levothyroxine alone without surgery.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—completion surgery with nodal dissection is preferred.
items.reference: Case 5: "Extranodal extension is considered a risk factor for structural disease recurrence"

items.assertion: Assertion: Low-risk FVPTC after lobectomy does not require completion thyroidectomy.
items.reason: Reason: Because contralateral lobe and nodes were normal without high-risk histology.
items.explanation: Both true for Case 3—completion and RAI not recommended.
items.reference: Case 3: "completion thyroidectomy or radioactive iodine therapy (Answer E) would not be recommended."

items.assertion: Assertion: Thyroglobulin antibodies interfere with Tg immunoassay interpretation.
items.reason: Reason: Because TgAb always indicates active recurrence.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—TgAb interferes with assay but does not always mean recurrence.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "The presence of thyroglobulin antibodies interferes with the thyroglobulin immunoassay and interferes with the interpretation of serum thyroglobulin as a tumor marker."

items.assertion: Assertion: Patients meeting RAI criteria after initial lobectomy should undergo completion thyroidectomy.
items.reason: Reason: Because RAI can be given effectively with a full thyroid lobe remaining without surgery.
items.explanation: Assertion true per guidelines; reason false—completion is needed for RAI in most protocols.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "If a patient meets criteria for radioactive iodine, then completion thyroidectomy ± central neck dissection is recommended."
